input.initialPrompt:
I'm setting up .NET MAUI development on Windows 11 from scratch. I need
to target both Android and Windows. I can't run commands right now — give
me a complete step-by-step plan with all the PowerShell commands I'll need,
including how to discover the right versions dynamically.

data.content:
Here's a complete setup plan for .NET MAUI on Windows 11 targeting Android + Windows.

---

## 1. Install the .NET SDK

```powershell
# Check if dotnet is already installed
dotnet --list-sdks

# Install via winget (discovers latest .NET 8 SDK dynamically)
winget search Microsoft.DotNet.SDK
winget install Microsoft.DotNet.SDK.8 --accept-source-agreements --accept-package-agreements

# Reload PATH
$env:Path = [System.Environment]::GetEnvironmentVariable("Path","Machine") + ";" + [System.Environment]::GetEnvironmentVariable("Path","User")

# Verify
dotnet --version
```

## 2. Install the MAUI Workload

```powershell
# List available workloads to confirm naming
dotnet workload search maui

# Install MAUI (includes android, ios, maccatalyst sub-workloads)
dotnet workload install maui

# Verify installed workloads
dotnet workload list
```

## 3. Install Java JDK (Android build dependency)

```powershell
# MAUI/Android needs JDK 17
winget search Microsoft.OpenJDK
winget install Microsoft.OpenJDK.17 --accept-source-agreements --accept-package-agreements

# Set JAVA_HOME (adjust path if winget installs elsewhere)
$jdkPath = Get-ChildItem "C:\Program Files\Microsoft\jdk-17*" -Directory | Select-Object -First 1
[System.Environment]::SetEnvironmentVariable("JAVA_HOME", $jdkPath.FullName, "User")
$env:JAVA_HOME = $jdkPath.FullName

# Verify
java -version
```

## 4. Install Android SDK via the MAUI workload tooling

The MAUI workload auto-provisions the Android SDK on first build, but you can control it explicitly:

```powershell
# Find where the Android SDK was provisioned (or will be)
# Default location:
$androidSdkPath = "$env:LOCALAPPDATA\Android\Sdk"
# If using Visual Studio, it may be under:
# C:\Program Files (x86)\Android\android-sdk

# Set ANDROID_HOME so CLI tools can find it
[System.Environment]::SetEnvironmentVariable("ANDROID_HOME", $androidSdkPath, "User")
$env:ANDROID_HOME = $androidSdkPath

# If the SDK isn't provisioned yet, a dotnet build will auto-download it.
# To manually accept licenses ahead of time:
& "$env:ANDROID_HOME\cmdline-tools\latest\bin\sdkmanager.bat" --licenses
```

## 5. Install the Windows App SDK (for WinUI3 / Windows target)

```powershell
# Discover available versions dynamically
winget search Microsoft.WindowsAppSDK

# Install the Windows App SDK runtime
winget install Microsoft.WindowsAppRuntime.1.6 --accept-source-agreements --accept-package-agreements

# Enable Developer Mode (required for sideloading/debugging)
reg add "HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\AppModelUnlock" `
    /t REG_DWORD /f /v AllowDevelopmentWithoutDevLicense /d 1
```

## 6. Create and build a test project

```powershell
# List MAUI templates to confirm they're available
dotnet new list maui

# Scaffold a new app
dotnet new maui -n MauiTestApp
cd MauiTestApp

# Build for Windows (WinUI3) — discovers target framework dynamically
$winTfm = Select-String -Path "MauiTestApp.csproj" -Pattern 'net\d+\.\d+-windows[\d.]*' |
    ForEach-Object { $_.Matches[0].Value } | Select-Object -First 1
dotnet build -f $winTfm

# Build for Android — discovers target framework dynamically
$androidTfm = Select-String -Path "MauiTestApp.csproj" -Pattern 'net\d+\.\d+-android' |
    ForEach-Object { $_.Matches[0].Value } | Select-Object -First 1
dotnet build -f $androidTfm
```

## 7. Run the app

```powershell
# Run on Windows
dotnet run -f $winTfm

# Run on Android emulator (must have an emulator AVD created)
dotnet build -f $androidTfm -t:Run
```

## 8. (Optional) Set up an Android emulator

```powershell
# List available system images
& "$env:ANDROID_HOME\cmdline-tools\latest\bin\sdkmanager.bat" --list |
    Select-String "system-images"

# Install a system image (pick an appropriate API level)
& "$env:ANDROID_HOME\cmdline-tools\latest\bin\sdkmanager.bat" `
    "system-images;android-34;google_apis;x86_64"

# Create an AVD
& "$env:ANDROID_HOME\cmdline-tools\latest\bin\avdmanager.bat" create avd `
    -n "Pixel_API34" -k "system-images;android-34;google_apis;x86_64" --force

# Launch the emulator
Start-Process "$env:ANDROID_HOME\emulator\emulator.exe" -ArgumentList "-avd Pixel_API34"
```

---

**Key notes:**
- Steps 1–4 are strictly sequential (each depends on the prior).
- The `dotnet workload install maui` step does the heavy lifting — it pulls Android build tools, SDKs, and platform dependencies automatically.
- If builds fail with SDK version mismatches, run `dotnet workload update` to align everything.
- For CI/headless scenarios, add `--accept-android-licenses` to your first `dotnet build` targeting Android: `dotnet build -f net8.0-android -p:AcceptAndroidSDKLicenses=true`.
data.reasoningText: The user wants a complete step-by-step plan for setting up .NET MAUI development on Windows 11, targeting Android and Windows. They want PowerShell commands and dynamic version discovery. Let me provide a comprehensive guide from my knowledge.
